metadata.description: Plants produce secondary compounds that humans use in medicine, cosmetics, sanitation, fine chemicals, pharmaceutics, perfumes, and nutraceutics. For example, several species of the genus Syzygium have antibacterial, antifungal, and antioxidant properties and that could be used against cancer and some neurological disorders. This study looks at the three species of Syzygium (S. malaccense, S. cuminii, and S. jambos) found on the island of Moorea, French Polynesia, to see if any of their secondary compounds can induce mortality in mosquito larvae. It also observes whether each species attracts different insect populations with different secondary compounds. It is shown that the distribution of insects between the three species is significantly different. It is also shown that S. malaccense killed 95% of the mosquito larvae after 24 hours, whereas the other two species did not kill significantly more than the control. Future studies may want to isolate the compounds in S. malaccense that actively kill mosqutio larvae.
metadata.title: The distribution, arthropod communities and larvicidal properties of three species of Myrtaceae on the island of Moorea, French Polynesia
